Hey, it's Andre and Sean. Today we are talking about getting started with the system. This is what we refer to this as the bridge building masterclass and it's really the idea is to understand where you are relative to where you want to go and then build a bridge between and build the metaphorical bridge between here and there. I'm going to start off with a quick overview that you see on your screen. Andre is sitting back relaxing probably having espresso. He's going to give me just enough rope to hang myself with and then he'll jump in and tell you the things that I should have said. And then we'll just see the conversation leads. None of these conversations are scripted. We work back and forth on the imagery. We understand we have conversations about what we want to talk about, but we're both just as excited to see what emerges. It's always fun. So we'll go left to right here. But having said that, I'm going to violate it immediately. I want to start in the middle first just to introduce an idea. You'll see there's an A and B on the screen. This is an idea that we have both learned from Robert Fritz. He's written some incredible books really. He's just a profoundly interesting thinker. And Fritz brings this idea of structural tension to the creative process. So between A, which is where you are right now, and then B, which is this desired future state, the idea that we're creating this natural tension, and it's the tension that pulls us toward that desired future state. And if we get that tension right, and Fritz refers to these as tension resolution systems, if we get that tension right, we're pulled from A to B. If we get it wrong, and Fritz would call that a resolving system, where the tension goes from one point to another naturally. Where we get that wrong is if we have what he refers to as an oscillating structure, which is the metaphor he uses. It's the car is the one the resolving gets you from A to B, the rocking chair, there's lots of motion, but you never go anywhere. It's sort of that oscillating back and forth. So if we have an A and a B, but we're pulled back toward A for some reason, so we make three steps forward, three steps back, we're trying to avoid that. That would be oscillation. There's a lot of motion, but not really any progress. So we'll get into the details about what that looks like and the precision required to get there in this conversation. But that central idea from Fritz is really, really, really important. Anything to add to that, Andre? And we're both long-term fans of Fritz and his work on structural tension. Yeah, I think that anything I'll add is we have written fairly extensively about Fritz and what you're seeing in front of you. So as part of this, we can link out to at least there's two long emails that are carved on our site that we spoke about this in detail. So we can just link out to that if you want to go a bit deeper. But we're going to cover most of it now anyway. Yeah, that's interesting too, because we are Fritz's such a deep thinker, but the A to B tension, we also create that for our readers. We talk about that extensively. But there's also we can use it for ourselves. It's kind of like a Swiss Army knife of utility, because if we structure it well for our audience-facing material, it pulls the audience's attention forward. When we structure it equally well for our internal thinking for a planning, it pulls our attention forward and our action forward. So it's just a profoundly interesting idea. All right, so you'll see on the far left here, we have this, there's a three-part drawing of this audience offered in economics. Now, if you ask 99 out of 100, sorry, if you ask 100 skilled marketers, if you boil it all down to what does it come down to, what remains, what's irreducible, and what you'll generally hear is some variation of audience and offer. If you have those two things, you have a business. Our dear friend Jack Bourne created many years ago. I think he called it the profit triangle. But Jack added this third component, which is economics. If you have an audience and an offer, but the economics don't work, meaning you're spending $3 to earn $2, that doesn't work. We call those small-fortune businesses. You have to start with a large fortune, and then you end up with a small fortune. But these three components, once we understand them, you realize they're essentially irreducible. And that's really important because if we just said to you, hey, let's have you figure out what's your current reality, what's objectively true, just describe it to us, you wouldn't really know any place to start. You could tell us a million things. You could spend hours or days. And what the audience offer in economics does is it gives us a place to put our stake in the ground to say, okay, let's work through these, each of these systematically. So for example, when we're talking about current reality, the first question we want to know is, do you have an offer? And it's not to get into weeds here. But the offer that we made in early February 2022, with the first time that we presented it to our audience, that offer, it contained the product, but the offer was different. It was the lowest price we would ever offer it, for example. There was a certain amount of time when that was available. And there were there were lots of things, it was the people who joined with the first group. And there are lots of things that made that an offer. So the first question we want you to answer to orient yourself to your current reality is, do I have an offer? And do I have more than one? I mean, it's, one of the things we've talked about in the past, I think, is this idea of plan the verb versus plan the noun. You know, plan the noun is you have this written document that is, this is going to happen in their checklist and all these things. I mean, if that lights you up and it works for you, great. But plan the verb, like the act of planning, that's where so much of the value is. It's the thinking through the planning. And yes, the execution, but it's, don't get caught up in like, okay, I think six weeks is an ideal timeframe. And in six weeks, I'm going to have this incredible, you know, new reality and six weeks arrived and only got 20% of what I wanted to do. I mean, there's actually, there's an entire bias, you know, psychological cognitive bias called the planning policy. Like you're not, you're not alone. We routinely as a species cannot accurately plan things from a time perspective and what we'll accomplish. So the first, I guess the place to start with your desired future state is to, is to try to under, try to really understand for you, what is it, what timeframe feels exciting. Then there's some natural rhythms to planning annually is good. Annual, you know, I would recommend everybody not start off with an annual plan annual is, it's too long. It's your too detached from the outcomes. It's too easy to get behind. You don't get the immediacy of the payoff. Another, you know, another common planning, you know, increment is a week, I think a week is, week is very tactical. You can get things done in a week and you can see progress, but it's not, it's not the inspirational level of, you know, okay, I'm going to be in this like pulled toward this new reality. It's probably somewhere, the ideal planning timeframe for the broadest group of people listening to this is probably somewhere between a month and maybe a quarter at the far end. I would initially recommend, I'm curious to hear what you're going to take on this as to, I would recommend somewhere in the neighborhood of like four to six weeks, which to me feels like a natural, like higher level A to B planning. A month to six weeks feels, feels like a sweet spot to me. I'm curious where, what feels like a sweet spot for you? Yeah, I think, I think there's, there's two insights, two insights that I had, and one of which is that the duration will probably change or shift as the business is at a different phase or stage or, you know, or maturity. So, you know, if you've got zero here, so you, so you're going into the durable business, you know, literally everything you do is, is going to be, it's going to create some positive impact, whereas later on when the business is more mature and you're just tweaking dials slightly, that, that timeframe is likely going to be different in some meaningful way, depending on how many people are on the team, how many people are working to execute on what that, what that be looks like. And what you just mentioned is really important too, is that if you're, if we use the marathon example, and if you train for a marathon, and you know, let's say for the sake of argument that you give yourself 18 months, that you're currently running on 5k on weekends with friends, and you say, you know, I loved in 18 months from now, I would love to be able to run a marathon, complete a marathon. Don't worry about the time or whatever you just want to complete a marathon in 18 months. When the, when the, if you, assuming the training goes successfully and you put the time and effort in, when you, at the end of race day, you're now someone who has run a marathon, right? That's, that's orders of magnitude, five orders of magnitude different than the person who, who ran the 5k and was aspiring to the marathon. Well, now when you think about, okay, what's the next thing that I want to achieve, you're standing on an entirely different platform. You are now someone who has completed a marathon. That doesn't mean you now need to run an ultra or whatever, but it may be the twice a year you run marathons. I mean, it's, it's, but it's, it's the idea that you just communicated, which is as you make progress, progress becomes your new reality. And that's the entire cycle here. It never stops. Unless you choose to, you know, sort of step off the, from ergo around at some point, that every time you make progress, you are, you're a very different person. It's that idea about you, you can't step in the same stream twice. So stream's not the same and you're not the same. And this, this A to B process, this pulling forward, I think the only distinction that I would add there is, I don't, I don't know if Fritz, it, it may be, I don't know if it's just my interpretation, but I, what I didn't get a sense for it from him was as much about the, the finality of it, the clean break of the B, although now that I think about it, because he focuses so much on creative projects, I guess there is, there is a, there is an actual resolution. And that, that seems to the point that you made, that does seem important that, that the A to B journey has resolved. You can say, I have now achieved this desired future reality, or I've reached the point in time I gave myself to do it, and I'm going to reassess my current reality and then create a new desired future reality to pull myself toward that. What we don't want, and this, this happens enough that we want to caution you against it, what we don't want is A to be vague and B to be vague, so that you're not quite sure where you are, you're not really sure where you're going, and when you get there, it won't necessarily be clear that you've arrived. If that's a really, if you want to create a perfect recipe for not making progress, that's probably it, those are probably the ingredients that you need to not make progress, because it, it won't feel exciting, it just won't. Yeah, I'll add one extra thing, because as soon as I understood what Fritz was trying to do here, which is what we're trying to explain to you here between A and B, it was such a revelation in the sense that if someone's, if someone's business, they have no offer, they have no audience, they essentially at zero, so their path through, through our system would be, would be the general business. Now, well, then you go into the general business, and the whole idea behind the general business is to go from zero to 100,000, right? So that's, that's roughly, well, it's from zero to something, but, but ultimately, you want to get to 100,000 would be the first big thing, right? Well, B is not 100,000. This is not zero, and that's 100,000 when you look at this thing that we're just explaining to you now, if you go into this the first time, B isn't the, isn't anything close to 100,000. B could be, could be, you want your page, you want a landing page built, you want an opt-in page built, you want your emails written, and you want to get, get your first opt-in. That's B, and that maybe is a, is a six-week sprint. That's B. B is nothing to do with 100,000. And when you think about it that way, it, it almost removes overwhelm, because when you're looking at this, at this nebulous thing in front of you, and it's like, how the hell am I going to get to 100,000 or 1 million or 10 million or whatever that, that, that fictional number is that you want to try and get to you. That is never B. So I think I just wanted to draw your attention to that. And as soon as you get that, it just, it's so liberating, because, you know, when you, when you run to get to B, like a few weeks into the future, and you pulled out all your assets, and you put it there, and you put this thing lab, and you get your first opt-in, that's such an amazing feeling, because now, now A becomes that. So your current reality is now, you have all these assets in place, you have a, you have a very basic system that is generating leads, and that's amazing, right? So then you will then define, okay, what's B now? Maybe B at that point is 500 people opting in, because, you know, you want to increase your certain things, your, your metrics or your, your lead flow coming into A. So yeah, I just wanted to say that. So B is not this, this, this nebulous thing way into the future. It's always a smaller goal. Yeah, and you just drew, you just drew attention to a distinction, I think is really important, which is paying attention to your internal GPS about what motivates you as an individual. Yeah, there's, and I, in broad strokes, very, very broad strokes, there's, there's a spectrum of, I don't even know what I would describe it, but certainly there's a spectrum of thought and a spectrum of research and a spectrum of, really, I think just what we're drawn to. And on the one hand, and I'll get a lot of this wrong, at the detail levels, I'll just ignore that. But on the, on the one hand, you have the set the bar so low that it's impossible not to achieve it. I think of this as like the tiny little habits, the BJ fog stuff, the, and research backs that up. Like if you, if you, if you can bring the, the, if you can, and this is an idea from, I think Charlie Munger has mentioned this with a he and Warren Buffett that they, you know, I think the phrase used to sound like they never tried to jump over, you know, climb tall fences, they just stepped over, like they kind of stepped over the low fences, you know, and did the easy stuff and just consistently did the fundamentals that were easy. Like that's, that's one side of, of the, of a spectrum. And that side, it seems to, if I, if I'm understanding the research and the idea as well, that seems to work for a lot of people. That if you just make the actions easy enough, and you stack them up, it's sort of, it's consistency over intensity. And you just, you do the thing and you make the thing so easy to do that you kind of can't help but do it. And then you chain together a series of those easy things that eventually you wake up one day and you're doing things that from the initial perspective were hard, but now they're easy because you've made them so easy to get there. The other side of that spectrum is this idea that there, there's a certain threshold at which we're not, we want to get engaged in some, it's almost like something has to rise to a certain level where it's interesting enough that we'll do the work. I fall into that camp. I'm not the, the tiny little habits guy. The, I can't stand, I understand it intellectually, but I have zero interest in it. It just does not motivate me. So if I tried to make a, a shorter timeframe, desired future reality that involved me barely doing anything and finding myself six weeks later, having achieved this thing that was through the sum of me barely having to do, it just doesn't excite me. I just won't do it. It's, it's oddly demotivating to me. I need to put something, I need to have something that's that, for whatever reason in this, I think they're, I'm certainly in the minority, but I mentioned this just so that each person listening to pay attention that the, there is, there's no right way to do this part of the planning. There's only the way that's right for you. Meaning if you, if you go through this exercise and like, okay, I think, you know, I'm going to try to create a plan for a month and I'm going to be very conservative about what I can accomplish. And I want to make it so easy to get from here to there and you find yourself just not, it just doesn't light you up. Try, try, you know, what I like to do sometimes is like, I try to do something that seems a little stupid. Like that's why I decided to train for a triathlon. You know, for me, it was like, okay, well, I want to get off the couch. All right, great. What should I do? Wow, let's do a triathlon. It makes, it doesn't make sense from a, it doesn't make sense from on any dimension other than it was, it was extremely motivating for me to do that. So just pay attention to, to how, how you respond to that. It's really easy to get it wrong in either direction. It's really easy to make it too easy. And it's really easy to make it too hard. And both of those things can be demotivated. So you're looking for that Goldilocks sweet spot where you're like, you know what, that seems like if I get there, it's going to be exciting. Like that's exciting, but it's, it's not so overwhelming. It's not so scary that I'm not, that I, I was just abandoned all hope. And if you, if you want to add to that, because I know you're much more familiar with one side of that equation than I am. Yeah. That's, it's good you mentioned that. And if you, if you're in a team that's, that's larger than, than one person, it's good to, to, to send one person over the Bob wire fences, if, if that's lots, if that luts them up. So, you know, Sean goes over all the, all the taller fences with the Bob wire or the razor wire on the top and jump over the little fences. And we then, then we get to be, but Sean, Sean gets there. He's all torn up. And I'm, I'm still looking fresh like a daisy. Do you want to believe that? I'll, I'll allow you to have that fantasy. I will, I will, actually, I guess I'll say, I will not, I will not say anything to undermine that fantasy. All right. And you just mentioned something that's really important here, which is the planning dimension of team versus individual. We're going to, we're approaching this in a way that should be agnostic, meaning you could do this with a team where you could do this as an individual. If it's, if you're doing this as a team, from my perspective, you need to have an ultimate decision maker. And that, that person is the one who decides what B is at the end of the day. Now there can be, there can be different channel, like different areas of responsibility for team participants, their role in getting from A to B. And there can be a larger vision of like what you're trying to achieve as a team. Let's say you can say, environment that you are a business owner, you have four people who are contributing in various capacities to go from A to B. That, that's still the, there's, there's buy in, there's, there's agreement. Everybody understands where A is and everybody understands crystal clear where B is. The only added complexity is that each person needs to understand his or her role in the A to B progression, which is just just another layer of complexity, but it's the same process. The, the team should arrive at B, not, you know, individuals showing up there weeks apart. All right, so let's, let's talk about, we'll talk about B and then we'll introduce a couple of ideas that we haven't put on the list here that we'll, I think they're important. One sort of a framework to think about in the other is just a deaf, it's run to be aware of, we'll see where they show up, if they show up. Yeah, and we also, we also, we also need to, need to mention the elephant in the room, which, and it's in the, it's in the, it's in the third word of our, of our name for this course. And so at some point, I don't know if we should mention it now, what we optimize and fall, but you know, the, the essence of this entire thing, it's, it's this is acknowledging that, that business and, and, and the way that we look at marketing is, is, is through this lens of it being a system and a system has, has, has a, you know, the entire system is optimized for one thing. And so all these little pieces that we're doing, they're all subordinate to this, to this larger thing or dysfunction that we, that we're optimizing towards. And so at some point, we need to just unpack that a bit, because it informs everything else. Yeah, that's, that was one of the two things. So that's this idea. Okay. So yeah, well, yeah, let's, I don't want to do this. I, I think I know where we can put that. And then, well, I think we'll put it there and then we'll riff on it and see where it goes. So I know, I know where, I think I know where it belongs. I have no idea where it's going to go after that though. It may just weave in thinking about it. So what, what you'll see here in the drawing is, is the red around these three, really these three critical questions, because what we want to know is the Delta, what's changed, right, between current reality and desired future state, what has changed around the dimensions that matter, what has changed with our audience or audiences, what has changed with our offer, offers, and what has, what's different about the economics of that. That's, that's like, that's a really good place to start. And this happens really across two dimensions to think about. One is, you know, more versus better, and it doesn't have to be either or. So more could be, let's say, I think this is an example you used earlier, it could be an increase at number of quality prospects on an email list. So we could say, okay, right now, what's to say for the sake of argument that you have done an initial audience, or initial offer to, you built an audience of a thousand people over months, once you reached that threshold of a thousand people that you've built this relationship with, you made an offer for a individual, essentially a, a live version of something that you want to turn into a course, you did, and then, and you created that offer, and you sold it to 20 people. And then you delivered on that. And now you've, you've stepped back and said, okay, my current reality is I have a thousand people on an email list of those thousand 20 became customers, I have 20 customers, I've delivered this course, I now have an eight module course that I created. And what I would like to do now is offer that live again, but I would this time, and I had, and let's just say for the sake of argument, say that you also have a waiting list with 50 names, but you, you want to get, let's, I don't know, we'll say, instead of having 20 people for the second time you offer it, you want to expand that to 100. And you've done the math in your head, and it comes down to that you need, you think if you've got, if you add another thousand, the high quality prospects to your email list combined with your waiting list and other things that you think that could get you to 100 people. And you, and then you think to yourself, I think if I do that at this pace, I can, based on, you know, all these factors, I think I can do that in 90 days. So now you can see how the process is starting to work. But you're looking forward at 90 days, it's informed by data, like this is where I am. I've done these things, I've got these results. This is, this is my current reality. But this is what I would like to be different along those same dimensions. Instead of having a thousand leads and 20 customers and 50 people on a waiting list, I would like to have 2000 prospects on an email list who I'm engaging with. And I would like to have 120 customers total, the first 20 and 100 more. And, you know, the economics, maybe the first time you offered it, it was $497. And the second time you offer it, you realize, you know, now that you get feedback and it was excellent and on all, now you're going to offer it for $997. So now you know the economics, 100 people, $997 minus your fees to deliver, you're somewhere in a neighborhood of $90, $95,000. Okay, like you do all of that, like, but you can, what I'm hoping you're hearing in this is the precision and the clarity, not of how you're going to do it, but of what the future reality, and this is such a critical distinction, do not get wrapped up in how, how is part of the planning process, how takes care of itself later, how is you're working back from the crystal clear vision of the future. If you start asking yourself how you're going to do it, while you're thinking about what you're going to do, it, it, it short circuits everything because then you think, well, I'm not, I've never done that before. Who cares? Right? It doesn't, that doesn't matter. All that matters is you know where you are and where you want to go. Now at the same time, that doesn't mean being, I don't want to say stupid, I do want to say stupid, doesn't mean being ridiculous. Where you say, oh, I did, you know, I have a thousand people and 20 customers, 90 days from now, I want 10,000. And it just sounds ridiculous. But I've heard the equivalent of this more times than I can count where it's like, okay, I've got a thousand prospects, 20 customers, 50 people on a waitlist, 90 days from now, I want 1500 customers. And you're like, great, I might not ask for a unicorn too, because it's more likely you'll get the unicorn. So it's not, we're not suggesting don't be too realistic, but we're also not suggesting that this is not, you know, no offense to anybody. This is not woo, woo, the secret vision board stuff where, just because you've put it out in the universe, the universe is going to deliver. Maybe, maybe, you know, I have no evidence to the contrary. But experience suggests that there's a sweet spot in between those two, where, where there's a, there's something between where you are and that enormous vision that you have for the future. There's something that can happen to get you closer to that in the next 90 days, that is both reasonable and a stretch. And it, it, it's somewhere in that neighborhood that feels like, like maybe it's a little, little outside the possibility range, but not so far that it's impossible. It's exciting. But it's not, it's not, doesn't feel impossible. It just feels like, wow, that's, that's going to be a challenge. That's what we're looking for. But it's the, what you want to be able to describe, and I mentioned this, and I'm going to, then I think, I think what we'll do is I'll, I'll wrap this up, hand it over to you for comments on this stuff, and then introduce the system stuff and the throughput and the other metaphor. So I, what, I really wanted to have people listening to this, watching this focused on, is can you describe the desired future state, the change, and the same things that you described with precision in your current reality? So when you describe with precision, the details of your current audience, if any, your current offers, if any, in the economics of that, when you imagine a point in time in the future, can you describe the change in those dimensions with the same level of precision? So it's not, I'll have more customers in 90 days. Is that one more customer? Two more customers? Or is it a hundred more customers? Like it's precision. It's precision between A to B, and it's that precision and that clarity. The Heath Brothers in their book, Switch, called this a destination postcard. You need to be able to see the postcard in your mind. Where are you going with precision? And you can say to yourself, I'm going to a place where instead of a thousand prospects, I have 2000 on my list, where instead of 20 customers, I have 120 customers. That's where I'm going. Instead of having put $8,000 in the bank, I'm going to put $95,000 in the bank. That's what you want with this precision, because that's what starts to pull your attention forward. All right. Anything to add to this before we jump into throughput and system stuff and really get excited? Yeah. There's no one way or right way to identify and prioritize opportunities. So I just wanted to unpack better, for example. So Sean's done the more example. Better in this case could be versions of your 1000 prospects, maybe in Sean's example, you generated 20 customers and then later you try to generate 100 customers extra or more. Well, maybe you only generate two customers. Or maybe those 20 customers was way less than you thought you should have done. So there's this weird delta that didn't happen. So better in that case could be you identifying that actually my traffic source seems fine. I think that that part's okay. And it's then prioritizing on maybe your assets on your website, you know, your offer, for example, or what you're doing to take a prospect and warm them up through a process that when they come onto your email list and then there's a few emails, by the time that offers put in front of them, they, you know, they way, way, way more more receptive to this offer. So better in that case could be doing things on your website and in your email and doing it in such a way that you now can maybe generate 50 customers in that first, you know, cohort, as opposed to the 20. So that's the better component. So again, there's no right or wrong way. Ultimately, you need more and you need better. And you're going to shift between your focus between these two things all the time, because you always want to try and get your, you know, you're always trying to get better customers, you're always trying to make better prospects. And that's the better part. But then you also need them all because, you know, if you're not increasing your lead flow, your business is never going to go anywhere. Right. So it's just this, this, the cycle between more and better over and over again, as you prioritize different opportunities in your business. And as the business goes bigger, there's going to be so much more opportunities to try and focus on will we'll scroll down on a little image just now, and you'll see what the thing looks like when you really zoom out to the 10,000 foot view and looking down into business. And you can see this and you know, then you're looking at all these opportunities. So yeah, I just wanted to add that extra piece. Yeah, and I think this is, this is probably an area where I think we probably disagree in this area a little bit, which is fun. I think, I think there's this misconception. Anyone who's been on a Q&A call knows this isn't true. But there's probably a misconception because people see our polished final work where we've worked through the stuff that you and I just always agree on everything, but we don't. And that's part of the dynamic of our relationship. We just see things. We have two, we both have similar length experiences, different enough that, that we just, we pick up different aspects. So it's fun for our audience to see both the things that we see together, where we have like full congruence and then where we start to emerge. And I think that are diverge, sorry, I had to think about system theory when I said emergent there. I think where I would caution on the better side is don't let better be an excuse for fuzzy. And I don't, you didn't, to be clear, you didn't say this, I heard it, which is, it sounds like the same thing, but it's not. We hearing something you were saying does not necessarily mean you said it as we, as we see when we, we send out emails and we get these 10 page replies that seem to have nothing to do with what we said in the emails that we sent. But the part I would caution on is when you, if you make better goals, that what better is has the same level of precision as more, more is objectively easier, just because, you know, as a good friend of mine says once a long, once Ross and we'll say, hey, last year we did 100,000, this year we did 700,000, he loves to blur down, hey, that's more. Like in the state, the obvious, so more is easy, more is easy to see, which means it's easier to be precise about when you do better, when, when you have a better goal, be cautious or be, be committed to precision in better. And there's, I don't think one is, is any worse than the other, more versus better goals, just understand exactly what it means. Because there are qualitative things that are hard to pin down, like you might have, you know, here's a, here's a good example of what I would consider a better goal versus more, although it's better expressed as more, or as less actually, which is, if you've ever had the experience as we've recently had of a long promotion with a lot of, of, of prep, then you know, you can, it's a lot of work, lots of weekends, lots of late nights, very demanding tempo, and you know, some people that's just, that's how they do business all the time, they have to be putting in 16 hour days, six and seven days a week, open ended. So if you could very easily have a current reality where, and it's not, you know, we don't have this dimension on here, but we could certainly add a fourth dimension, which is experience. What is your personal experience of your business? And does that need to be the thing that change, changes? Do you need, do you need, if you've been going too slow, do you need to do more? If you've been going too fast, do you need to slow down? So you could, you could very easily say, you know, my current reality is I have this author, I have this audience, the economics of XYZ, but my experience of the business is whatever it is. You can say, you can express it qualitatively, I feel burned out, like I'm working too much. And then, but then you get into some, you put some numbers on that. You know, five, you know, I'm working three to five nights a week, you know, past 10pm. The last X number of weekends, I have worked one or more days, like just get that precision and say six weeks from now, I'm really not going to do much to the author, I'm not going to do much to the audience. I'm not, the economics are, I assume will be kind of the same, but I want to have those three things be true. But I want to have one full weekend off, one full weekend day off, every, every week, one full weekend off, every month. And I don't want to work past five pm, more than one night a week. Like that's a, to me, like that just gets mixed in things, and that's better. It's a qualitative experience. But it's, it still has a more component. Does that make sense? You should see where I'm going with that. I think we're mixing up the examples here, but I hope that, I don't know if that makes sense at all. I think there's, there's, there's certainly different, different ways you can, you can look at this idea of more and better. And I think they're not meant to be one thing. It's, you know, better is better and more are both subjective. And, you know, I've, I've focused a lot of energy on better over the years. I focused on better more than, I focused on more over the years. And again, better for me is different to Sean's, you know, definition of better, perhaps. And, you know, just one way to look at better is you create, you create amazing assets on your websites that, although there's, there's a lot of time and energy and resources that have gone into create this thing. Once you've created it, it's created, it's, it's an asset that's, that's going to work for you. You know, it's like an Archimedes lever. It is now just so much better that it's going to make more better. It's going to make more better because, because better makes everything better. So now as you increase all the dials on, on the far left, so on your, on your audience piece, and if you've got traffic components pointing in, so you've got traffic engines or coming or pointing in here. And because you've, you've, you've done the work to make this, your website component better or your, or your front end better, more becomes just way better. It can be a, you know, be a proper step up as opposed to just trying to, trying to work on more and more and more the time to the, and just ignore better because then more can never become, it just won't scale in the same way because as soon as you, you turn off all those traffic engines, you've still got like a really bad front end. That's not that great, for example. And where it's better in those cases, it's, it's always going to be better. Well, not always, but you can go to, to the Wayback Machine or archive.org and you can look at pretty much any or most websites and you can go back one year, go back five years, go back 10 years, and you can see what, what it used to be and what it looks like now. And in many cases, it's just so much better, but that, that better took years, right, took years and years, but now their current reality is this thing that's just so much better. So funneling and more at that point, the whole thing is just going to work better. So again, more and better for me is, is a, is a thing that, that they need to work together. And one is a huge lever for the other. Yeah, I think that's, that's an extra thing I'll add. All right, so that's a good segue into, you know, we don't want to get into systems theory in detail yet. We want to talk about that in a moment, just sort of at a high level, but just to make it sort of approachable. But what is, what is really important to, to understand in the context of where you are versus where you're going is a concept, a concept from systems theory, which is, you know, the term is throughput and throughput is the rate at which a system produces the system goal. And that sounds like a mouthful, like, well, wait, I said, now I've got like a million things, wait, there's a system and it has a goal and then there's the rate. Okay, it sounds infinitely confusing. But it's actually the, what makes it so magical and so useful is that throughput is the single measure by which we evaluate progress. And, and throughput, it needs to be the most meaningful, it needs to be the way that you know you're improving. It needs to be the single metric that matters, that all decision making is coordinated to. Our measure of throughput is happy customers. It's not customers. Because, and the distinction is very important. And actually, Andre, I have very different definitions. We have very similar definitions, but then very different approaches to how we implement those and think about those definitions. And I think it'll be interesting if we both go down that road in a second or not. Maybe I'm just, I'm teeing up something that's not going to be interesting at all, but from a, the meaningful distinction for us is we're not in the customer at any cost business or the customer at all costs business. We are in the happy customer business. And happy customers is there's, there are objective components to it, where I tend to put more focus and there subjective components where I also, I put, I think I put less focus on that relative to Andre putting the focus of it. Maybe not. We'll both sort of talk through this in a second. But what, what's important about that distinction is because that the single most important metric in our business is happy customers, it informs lots of decision making that would be different if the metric were just customers. So here's an example that probably everybody who's listening to this is experienced. If you've been, if you've, if you've ever been in any of our enrollments or our promotions in the last couple of years, you'll see that we send them the final day we send an email, one email, you never get from us a countdown, four hours to go, one hour to go, last call, you never see us do a, oh, the link, we've got some feedback that the link was broken. So we're opening up enrollment for another 24 hours. We never do any of that. And if we did do that, we would generate more customers, absolutely, categorically, no doubt in my mind, we would generate more customers doing that. But more customers, we're not optimizing for that. We're optimizing for happy customers. And we've made the decision that all of those things to get just more customers aren't actually in service to generating happy customers. We just don't do it. The throughput for us is it's different, which means it's, it's that happy customer, the customer who objectively doesn't refund, at least you can't be a customer at all, never mind a happy customer if you don't no longer a customer. They're people who are consuming the content, they're people who are engaged, they're people getting value from it. They're people who they send us emails or communicate with us, they become fans and super fans and happy customers, a big spectrum. It's, there's a lot there. But the most, the initial objective way to determine, it's sort of an exclusionary thing is after 60 days, how many people remain? That's the first thing to look at. So if you, and that's refund rate, because if let's just say for the sake of argument that you get your business going, and you get to build the perfect mousetrap, and you're really good at selling, you're really good at generating excitement. And you get 100 people every month who, excuse me, become customers. But 60 days later, only 80 of that 100 remain, your refund rate is 20%. So now, where I think Andre and I get a little bit different is do we still consider those 80 happy customers? I consider at least that they have to be there to be a happy customer. The 20 who left are clearly unhappy customers. They're not customers anymore. But it's that it's the 80 that matters. It's recognizing that it's not the number of people who buy, it's the number of people who stay. That's critically important because if you're optimizing, is you can, you can, you can optimize for the buyer at the expense of the person who stays. And it's very counterproductive. We do that. So we use this single metric. We encourage others to borrow it. I don't think we invented it. Maybe you invented it. I don't know. But it's, we're not protective of it. But this idea that the single most important metric in your business is the happy customer and making decisions from that perspective. Is this likely to generate a happy customer versus just generate a customer versus not do anything at all? Like that, that single metric then gets, then it becomes the thing against which you measure every initiative. More and better, doesn't matter because is it driving that number? Is it making that the whole reason the business is the business? The whole reason the system is the system is to create happy customers. And if you're doing something that is, that is not making customers happier or more customers happier, why are you doing it? That's the question, which is going to lead us to the next part. But I want to hand it over to you for a while to riff on that. Yeah. I mean, it's, it's, it's, it's so easy to go wrong here when you don't understand this, this, this higher order definition of the system is, is, you know, there's, there's a single goal that the system itself is being optimized for. And then you can zoom in to the parts that essentially create the business. And I'm sure everyone's heard of this, this, these ideas that it's, it's, it's taken from, from economics and then apply to business, whereas if you just increase your, your clicks on your ads by 10% and then just increase your, your clicks on your website from page one to page two by 10% and just increase your opt-ins by 10, and it's increasing all these things. And then don't worry because now you're going to have this business that produces exponential growth. And there's, that's the biggest lie in the world that, that idea can't, you know, that idea borrowed from economics cannot be applied to business because business isn't, isn't sales on a, on a spreadsheet. It, it, it doesn't compound in the same way. And when you, when you optimizing in that way, trying to optimize all the little parts for this, for the highest throughput, like each part you want to optimize for the highest throughput, it undermines the objective of the entire system. So the system itself gets worse because you're trying to make and force all the little parts in between to be optimized to the max. You know, it's so easy to, to get more clicks on an ad by, by making big claims and get, get, get more people to opt in by sticking in a huge big brab. And then doing all these things, you know, increasing, like really ramp up and ratchet up the, the sales pressure by adding urgency and doing all these things. But that does not contribute to, to the, to the end goal. I mean, I've seen many, I've had opportunity of looking inside of businesses that are in doing these huge product launches. And it is not uncommon that there's a, there's a between a 20 and a 50% refund rate on some of these, these big launches. I mean, 50% a few years ago was, was, was just, you know, part of the course because of all these different things that, that needs to happen along the way. So what we're saying is that when you understand and realize that you optimizing for happy customers, if that's, if you create your definition of what a happy customer means, and then you work backwards from that, and then at each little point. So on your opt-in page, on your, on your ads, you keep thinking back, is this going to contribute to that, to that, to the goal of the system? And if it's no, well, then you don't do that. So, you know, one of the insights of systems theory is by increasing all these little parts along the way, increasing the throughput of each little thing, it almost will always undermine the, the system goal. So just having that in the back of your mind and just asking yourself that question at every point, is this going to increase or decrease that system goal whenever you doing anything? Yeah. And I suppose we can introduce a theory of constraints here, but I don't want to get overly complicated and then you've won glazes. Go ahead. Yeah. And it's, I mean, it really comes down, it's a filter. And this is a good, a perfect segue into the next, to, I guess, it's a metaphor as a way to think about it. But the, we've mentioned this many times, the book, the title says everything. The book is good, but the title kind of says it all. And the title of the book is, Will It Make the Boat Bill Faster? It's by Ben Hunt Davis. He was in the British Men's Eight, who won, that's the British Men's Eight rowing team after, I don't know, 96 years or so. They hadn't won gold at the Olympics. They had a disastrous, or they had a subpar performance at Germany and the World Games. They got together as a team and made the decision that they wanted to win gold in Sydney, in the 2000 Sydney Olympics. And they did. And the way they did is they subordinate subordinated every decision they made to a single question, will it make the boat go faster? And if the answer was no, they didn't do it. And that's, to me, is the best definition, the best metaphor and description of the idea of system throughput. It's the best one I've ever heard because if you're going to do something, say you sit down and you've gone through this process to reorient us to the A to B, or you sat down and you say, okay, here's my current reality. And here's what I want to do six weeks from now. This is what I want to have accomplished for these reasons. Okay, will it make the boat go faster? Will the things that you've decided to do increase the throughput of your system, the single number that matters more than anything else? Will that number change in any way whatsoever? If the answer is no, why are you doing? Now, the first thought someone might have is, well, it's not just about making more money or it's not, that doesn't have to be the definition of the boat going faster. That doesn't, your definition of throughput, like for Andre and I, we have a Venn diagram that we use. The Venn diagram, the sort of three components are, is the business creating value for us? Like, is it, you know, making money? Is it producing value for us? Are we creating, that's one circle, are we creating value for others that we feel good about? And then that's a second circle. And the third circle is, is our experience of the work positive for each of us as individuals? Do we like what we're doing? And where those three overlap, where we're making money, we enjoy what we're doing, we're creating value for an audience, that is the sweet spot. That is our boat going fast. That's the throughput for us. The objective measure for the business that tells us we're doing that is, is our happy customers, not just the customer, because we could, we could make more money if we only focused on one of those circles at the expense of the others. Is there, our experience of the business doesn't really matter, whether or not we're really doing a lot for our audience, doesn't really matter. We just want to jam, jam, jam, put a million dollars in our, in our hand in the next couple of months. But that's, that's not what a Venn diagram is supposed to communicate. The Venn diagram is supposed to show you that perfect point at which things that matter intersect. And that is the boat going faster. That's what it means. So if we were to sit down, for example, and say, okay, our current reality is this, and we, in the future, we want to, six weeks from now, we want to be working a lot less. And we look at our Venn diagram, and, and it doesn't, and, and experience isn't in there anywhere. It doesn't matter. Then it's not making the, it's not making the boat go faster. It's not, we haven't really made an improvement. And this, this idea of will it make the boat go faster is such, it's just a sentence to repeat to yourself to check in and ask yourself all the time, are the things that I'm doing, can I connect them to the things that matter? That like, that's what this question is all about. Because what I've certainly seen in my experience working with clients is the number of decisions that get made that have no relationship to anything improving whatsoever, or the system improving, doing what it's supposed to do better. I'm, I'm, I'm still shocked by the number of conversations I've been in where someone could not explain to me how all of the time and the energy and the dollars they were thinking about allocating to an initiative had any relationship to making that system better or making the boat go faster. And it was almost like there are many times when it was almost like no one had ever even considered that. And that is the most important question to ask ourselves over and over and over. Why am I going to do this thing? I'm looking out into the future and I want, I want this to be true. I want to be drawn to it. I want this to be my new reality. How does that relate to what it is that I'm actually trying to do here? What is meaningful to me in what I'm doing? How does it relate? And if the answer is I don't know, why the hell would I ever think about doing it? Because it's not, there's an opera, for everything we do, there's an opportunity cost. An opportunity cost means it's, it's not just the things that you do that matter. It's the thing that things that you do. I think I've lost you. Lisa there. I'm still here. Can you hear me? Oh, there we go. Yeah, yeah. You just disappeared for a second. Yeah, the power just flashed. So it's always entertaining when we're in the middle of something. All right. So I'll let you riff on that while I'm at it. Yeah. So I think the only thing I'll add to that is I'll highlight boat in that sentence because everyone's boat is going to be defined differently and this metaphor may sound obvious, but it's something that I think of all the time in the context of will it make the boat go faster? And if you're in the Formula One business, boat is the Formula One car. Sean has a tractor and he uses his tractor on his huge part of land to do tractor stuff. I don't know if he plows, but he certainly scoops snow with it and moves logs. Well, sticking slick tires onto his tractor isn't going to make the tractor go faster in that context. And if your definition of boat is you're into touring cars and while you're going long distances, well, building a Formula One car isn't going to get you there any faster. The car is going to blow up along the way. So I know that that sounds a bit obvious and a bit weird, but we all create metaphors that resonate with each of us, but that's one of the things often helpful is just define what boat is and it's not a Formula One car unless you're in the Formula One car business. And yeah. So are you back yet, Sean? Yeah, I'm here. And it's what you just said is really critically important to think about too when you're mixing parts. This is why we can't be in the parts business. You can't have, I think we have where we mentioned it was in the traffic engine or someplace, but when we talked about systems theory, you don't want to have parts from one type of a system bolted on the parts of another because the central insight of systems theory is how the parts relate to each other. It's the relationships that drive the action, not the parts themselves. And that's such a critical insight. So it's not the ad that somebody clicks on that's as important as the ad that somebody clicks on relative to what they see next relative to what their experience is days later. It's those relationships that matter. So if you have, and we see this happen all the time where somebody has like a franken business, right? Where you've got the super curiosity, click baby ad. And then that leads to a landing page that has lots of good content. And it's like, well, wait, what's just happened? What's going on here? Or then you might get the super quick baby ad and then the super short content with all the bolt, you're going to get all these things, these freebies, and then they get the freebies. And then there's a 10-part email series to get them to a $2,000 offer. In what world did any of that in total make sense? Like what led somebody to believe that if you could just get somebody to click on a goofy Google display network, click baby ad with a bold promise of curiosity that somehow 10 days later, they'd hand over a couple of thousand dollars. It just doesn't make sense. But you see it all the time. And when you start optimizing for those parts, and this is what you were talking about earlier, that's so profoundly important, is you can make any of the parts increase exponentially, but then the whole system gets worse. You could add the classic example is you could add a tremendous amount of traffic of ultra low quality and actually make your sales worse. It's just it's profound how many mistakes can be made by trying to focus on just a part. Yeah, totally. Alrighty. So, yeah, let's move on to the big drawing here. But before we do that, let's wrap up the A to B thing with the two questions that are going to segue into the really where everybody listening to this is going to spend 99.99999% of your time engaging with the system outside of the community itself, which is once you once you've gone through this process, you've and to be clear, this planning process, if you want to think about it this way, this visioning process, if that lights you up more, whatever it is, this understanding current reality relative to a desired future state, that process is something that you're going to do over and over and over again for the rest of your life, certainly the rest of your professional life. And it's something you're going to get so much better at, you're going to know yourself in the process much better. You're going to know, you're going to understand the dynamics of the process, what you can accomplish better, like all of those things are going to change. And that's just part of it. So if you do the first time you do this, if you feel like you just no idea what you're doing, it doesn't, it didn't get six weeks later, nothing seemed to who cares. If that's just you, the only way to get better at this is to practice it, and you're not going to be great at it, nobody is. So just do it, get out there, do it, make your best attempt about precision and clarity. But what you're going to find, think is when you once you have done this work, you know where you are, you know where you want to go, both of those are precise. And they're around these dimensions of audience and offer and economics and your experience of the business, you understand kind of what your boat is, like how like, what matters to you, what's your business, what does faster mean, what's the metric you're using for throughput, you're really going to have to answer two questions. Because now you know, you're going to know what needs to be done, right, to get from A to B, you're going to know, okay, I have X, I want Y between here and there, it's going to come down to two, two essential questions. The first one is do I know what to do to get from where I am to where I'm going, do I know what to do? And second, do I know how to do it? I don't think there are any other questions, right, you know where you want to go. And then the first question is, all right, well, how do I do that? How do I go from 1000 prospects to 2000 prospects in 90 days? And that's a what do I do question. And then the question becomes, how do I do that? And now I think we can segue down and show the broad overview of the system, so that we can orient everyone to this, really how to think about where to find those answers, the what to do questions, and then within the how to do it takes care of itself. Once you find out what to do, within the what to do is we explain how to do it. So, yikes, this is a terrifying image. I think what I'll do is I'm going to just, I'm going to hit one part of this, and then I'm going to hand over the rest of it to you to do the high level overview, so I can take a break from hearing my self talk. So the part that I'll focus on is the awareness engagement and conversion diagram at the top. This is, this for me was a, this is one of the most profound insights of understanding I had about systems theory. And Andre and I have been students of system theory for a very long time. It's something that intellectually influences my work greatly, my life greatly. I think about it a lot, but then I saw, I saw this, it's called a Kanitsa illusion. And once I saw that, I was like that, I finally understood conceptually something that was elusive to me. So the Kanitsa illusion that you'll see, there's, it looks like there's a triangle that's, that is labeled your business. But the reality is that that triangle doesn't, there, there, there was at no point did we draw a triangle. The triangle is an emergent property of the three gray shapes, little Pac-Man shapes, right? So the idea here is that what we think of as our business isn't actually a thing. There is not, there isn't something that is our business that we, that we can put in a box and we can take it to places and say, look, here's my business. What we have instead are we have activities through which we build awareness of our business or offer or us or whatever it is. We have activities where we engage with audiences where they take steps closer to us and we're building relationships with them. And then we have activities where though that in the awareness and the engagement are interacting in ways where that people become paying customers. They are, money is changing hands. And what we think of as our business that, that triangle in the center emerges from the way in which we do those things. Not those things are not something we do because of our business. Those are things that we do that actually create our business. And if we change any of those things, the whole business changes. That's the second component of this thing. If we said, okay, in the past for awareness, we did, you know, what we got, all of our, all of the awareness for our business came from organic search traffic on Google and people were actively interested and they were looking for us. And all of a sudden, for whatever reason, algorithm change or something that goes away. And now we're paying for traffic or we have affiliates or whatever, like the nature of the business changes. It's a, because awareness changed, the entire business is now different. And we may have to adapt the rest of the business to, to, to meet the change in the part of it, because the relationships of all the parts are what matter. And the way we've drawn this is very elegant, but you can imagine some franken shapes in there where it doesn't create an elegant looking business because the way you do one thing influences all the others and it looks really weird. So I love this Ken Eatson illusion. I love, I love what it communicates is this to me is the first time I felt like I really understood this idea of the emergence of the business from elements of the business, the relationship of those elements. One of them talks about this specifically, this idea that none of these activities exists in isolation. So awareness and engagement and conversion. There are probably some examples where, yes, let me click on it. They click on an ad, they buy a thing. But that's not generally how, how the world works. But the, the, you know, awareness and engagement and conversion are great labels, conceptual labels for broad categories. But a lot of the things that happen in your business, those, the, the edges and those are very blurry. Like the thing that makes somebody aware of you may by definition, peak their curiosity to get, to get engagement. Those two things may happen simultaneously when you, and that's, that's ultimately what makes the system so powerful is that, and this is, this is an idea that I hope we really get you to understand today that we haven't mentioned yet, which is the, because the system is based on a set of shared underlying principles, and those, and those principles are on a foundation of similar values. So the whole sit, it, it's an entire system where the parts are designed to be part of a larger whole, which sounds just sort of intellectual, but the, the net impact of that, which is so critical for everyone listening to this, and so critical over time to understand is that when all of the parts are designed to work together, when you, when you introduce one of them into the system, following into your business, following the ideas that we teach, the whole system improves because they're designed to work together systemically. So the way that you learn to, to drive traffic over which you have control, paid traffic in the traffic engine, the way that you learn to do that has a profound impact on, on engagement and conversion later. They're designed so that when, when they're put together, that you get a one plus one plus one is greater than three phenomenon, which is very different than going and buying a course on driving traffic, that may not have any relationship to the way that you're going to convert an audience to paying customers later. And that disconnect means that you could add, you could create 50 traffic campaigns that might not improve your business at all. But because of the way that, that what we teach works together systemically, when, when you are, when you're doing something anywhere in the business, the impact is to make your whole business better. That's the idea. And that points to something that's important to think about in your A to B planning is that you're probably not going to go wrong. So you're not, if you think, okay, you know, do I know what to do? I think it's this. And then you jump in and you do that, you know what it was, that wasn't actually, that, but that wasn't actually what I should have been doing there to get where I wanted to go. You haven't made a mistake. You've still likely improved your business, maybe not exactly around the dimensions that you've intended, but that you, you haven't gone down a road that you shouldn't have because every part of this is designed to work together to improve the whole there. And, and that's such a, it's such a hard concept to grasp, but it's such a powerful concept. So the fact that only Sean and I can create content for our entire business, no matter how big our business becomes, it is just the two of us creating the content, which creates, that's a constraint on the business, which is fine because we're aware of that constraint. So it's, and it also depends on where people are in their experience of actually going through planning. You know, for me, I know myself pretty well. I also, another thing I know of myself that I mentioned earlier is that I have to, the audacious isn't the right word, but I have to set goals for myself or I have to think about if I want to accomplish something, that's the mode that I'm in is accomplishment, then it has to be big enough to excite me, but not so overwhelming that I don't think I can do it. So I know, you know, I know those two things about myself, but I know them because I've gone through this dozens and dozens and dozens of times. If somebody else is listening to this, may not know these two things. And if you don't, those are things to pay attention to.
