Other people really orient well to a year, like a year is just the annual goal. I mean, it's, there isn't a right answer here, but there is a right answer for you and it's worth finding it. That's, that's a critical thing to keep in mind that this, the value of this process is in the, in what you get as a result and also who you become as a result, like how you know yourself, how you know how to know what you're able to accomplish, how to know what you like to accomplish. Those are the things. So that, that's the starting here to me. So what I would do, when I would, when I assess my current reality, one thing about me is probably people who listen to any of our calls, pick up on real quickly is, I am, I find objective, what I consider to be objective reality, it's very important to me and precision is very important to me. So I would probably err on the side of a much finer degree of precision about, you know, whatever current reality is. So let me actually sit down and look at the data, verify that my understanding of the data to establish this view of objective reality is true. And whatever narrative I'm telling myself about that, I want to check that too, because we're, I think it's fine to me, can't, I quote about how, you know, the worst thing we can do is fool ourselves and we're the easiest people to fool. Right. So I've spent quite a lot of time trying to learn the various ways in which I predictably fool myself and at least be aware that I might be doing it. So that finding the initial reality, run through that initial current reality, I would, I would want to have some level of precision. And I would know that I would be creating in my mind and narrative associated with that. And I would want to ask myself some questions about that. Like, do I know this? Do I think this? Am I applying some, am I projecting myself onto this situation, whatever it is, but I just want to be, I would be, I would be very, I would be, I would err on the side of precision current reality. And then when it, from the, for desired future state, I would set the bar a little higher than I'm, and felt comfortable so that it was motivating. And I would probably set that bar in somewhere in the neighborhood of six weeks. And I would ask myself questions around what do I want to be true six weeks from now across these various dimensions. And those dimensions would be, you know, certainly audience, offer, economics, my experience of it. I would want to double check with myself. Is, is my focus in this moment, is it more outcome, like accomplishment, achievement stuff? Or is it experience? Which of those two dimensions am I, am I, when I say two, I listed four, and I said two, the two dimensions, really it's, it's outcome versus experience. And outcome can be accomplishments, can be achievements, things like that. But then experience is like, what is my internal subjective experience of between now and then, and when I arrive there, like, how has that changed that? So I would ask myself a lot of these questions to get a pretty rich understanding of where I wanted to be. Because where I want to be needs to pull me forward. And in order for it to pull me forward, it needs to be big enough that it captures my attention, my interest, close enough that I feel like I'm going to get there reasonably quickly. So I can do the next thing that's exciting. And clear, this needs to, it needs to be crystal clear to me. What is it that I'm doing? Because I want to, I want to then ask that final set of questions for myself, which is, if this happens and I accomplish, if I actually do this, if this becomes true, do I care? Is it meaningful? And it's, that sounds like a ridiculous question with more of course it would be. I, at least 50% of the time that I go through this process, I realize that the thing that I am focusing on in this process that I think matters, isn't ultimately the thing that matters most. I mean, that's, that's a very common byproduct. And, and that's, that's a, it's a really good time to figure that out before you start taking action to realize, whoa, all right, everything that I said made sense. And how I got here, I can see it all, it makes sense. But if I actually get there, that doesn't feel like it's that mean. So let's go back, let's, let's figure out why. And then, then it's about, I might pressure test that a little bit. What's, what's going on here? I write every day. A lot of this stuff shows up in the writing. And it sort of helps point me in those directions. But at the end of that, whether it's hours or a day or whatever it is, I would want to have a very clear, and I love the thing I mentioned before from the Heath brothers in their book, Chip and Dan Heath, from their book, Switch, would like to have this idea of a destination postcard. It's just something that in my mind, I know where I'm going. You know, I know that where I'm going is it has these qualities. And so that I know when I get there. And it's exciting to me, like I want to go there. That sounds great. If six weeks from now, that's true. That's excellent. And it may only be that it's true and it's excellent because it's setting up the next thing, but that it's necessary to get there. That's fine. It doesn't, it doesn't have to be an end into itself. But it, it doesn't need to check those boxes. And at that point, that's when I then need to figure out, okay, now that, now that I know where I am, where I'm going, do I actually know what to do to get there? And this is the hardest part, certainly for me, has been the hardest part to say, okay, I, you know, we're making this change, or we're going to do this thing, or I want to accomplish this thing, to hit this new level, what, where do I need to go? So I'll use a real example here, just because this is something that's on my radar. One of the things we did last November, Honduran idea, when this, when these ideas for putting everything together in a system emerged, but when we were pressure testing that idea individually and together, I couldn't figure out the, really the dimensions around which to, to make that decision. And we didn't, and for me, that's very important. You can understand, like, how do we make this decision to go from a individual course business to a single course business? And I just, I wasn't figuring out ways to feel like my decision making was solid. So what I did instead, almost accidentally, they thought, well, let's just forget about all of them. If I just, if I just made a list of what a perfect business would include, what would, would the elements be? And I made the list, and it mapped really well to what we were talking about. Like I could see that the, that list included, you know, a customer generating front end offer, a core offer, continuity, a multi-level continuity. Like it had these things and then, then it makes, it made it so easy to understand how this decision we were making mapped to it. But part of that, in there, one of the things that's on my radar for, you know, probably in the next 90 days or so, is to create, co-create, to co-create and be responsible for the development of a customer generating front end offer. That the idea being that's a lower cost offer that we can pay for traffic and the amount we pay for traffic, the cost to acquire a customer is somewhere approaching break-even of the, what we make on the sale, the average order value. That's a break-even customer acquiring front end. That's a big project. Right. So let's assume that I've done the A to B. Let's assume that it's maybe mid-April, and I'm doing this exercise, and I know that my, what I need to do next, I know that we have a core offer. I know that we have continuity because we're releasing a lab at the end of March and lab elite. So I know those things are true. The next thing that I'll be focusing on beginning around mid-April will be working with Andre to develop this, this front end offer, this customer acquiring front end offer. So knowing that, that's my B. The B is, let's say, I want to give myself six weeks, and I want to have the actual offer so that we can then turn our attention to the traffic campaigns, et cetera. If that's the B point, A is where I am relative to that. It's the metric that I'm looking at is essentially offer an audience. Say that we don't have a, my current reality in mid-April will be that we don't have this front end offer yet. We do have a core offer, and we do have continuity. In our audience, we'll have some info about that. And then when I make that future reality, when I sketch that out in detail, I'm going to want to have a complete front end customer acquiring offer, and I'll put some dimensions around that. At that point, I need to go, okay, well, how do I know what to do? How do I do that? And what I will do, and this gets back to us, and we teach what we do, so it makes it really great when we need to do things, because we can just go to the material we've created as refreshers, because that really embodies our best thinking about it. But at that stage, I'm going to do, I'm going to essentially be what each of you, I'm a student of our own work. I'm going to go to the audience and offer Masterclass, and I'm going to work through the, not so much the first part, because we've had, those decisions are already been made for us, our business already exists. But the second part, using the value proposition design canvas, I'm going to do the exact same thing that we teach you to map out the dimensions of that front end offer, before I actually start working. And I'm going to use the audience and offer Masterclass to create the bones of that offer. Andre, I will co-create that together. When that's finished, so at the end of the six weeks or so, and that's that we have that offer we've created the content, and I have to answer, what am I going to do next? I'm going to go into the traffic engine. Unfortunately, I wrote the traffic engine, so it's embodied knowledge of myself, but I'm still going to essentially follow exactly what we teach in the traffic engine. I'm going to follow that for that next six weeks to build out the first campaigns, the way we teach them. So that's how I would orient to this. And then, just over time, I'm always going to find those parts in the system that map to the things that I'm trying to accomplish, and the how will be in that material. So that's two, two perspectives on that. Anything, we're coming up with just past two hours or anything. We want to add to wrap up or shall we call two hours successful today? Two hours and five minutes. Yeah, actually, let me end off on a little sideline story that may map to this. So our Bolta racing simulator during the first part of COVID, during, you know, while everyone was locked down, and it took me six months to build it to get other parts. And then I finally assembled this damn thing. And I've never been racing before. I've never raced on a track before. And I've got this thing. So obviously, the first thing I do is I jump into a really fast Porsche 911 RSR and also an open seat and I go tearing around various tracks. And I just, I can barely stay on the track. It's just ridiculous. And I, in that moment, I realized how difficult this is. And so me thinking about, well, my current reality at that point was, I know absolutely nothing about racing around a track. If the objective, if the objective is to get around the track in the lowest possible speed. And initially, I didn't really know B what B was because I was so new, I didn't know what I didn't know. So I went and did this, did this live racing course with with the next racing guy. And it was a group, a group thing. And it was over like six weeks. And each week, there was a different track. But the one, one of the constants across the entire training over the six weeks, is you prompt us into an into a Mazda MX-5, which if you, if you familiar with the car, it is slow. But it, but it's a mid-engine thing. So it, it is a really well-balanced car. It's slow on purpose because it then allowed him to teach us the fundamentals of driving. And because we weren't in some crazy fast car sliding around the corners, you know, the car con goes, con go faster than it can go. And it was one of the biggest insight was just like straight away learning that tearing around the corners with my foot pinned to the ground isn't going to get me around this track in the fastest time. And through that six-week process of him teaching us the, the principles of racing, you know, the fundamentals of, of braking and as weight distribution moves between the front and the back of a car and how to, how to fix grip and all those things done and done inside of a MX-5, which allowed us to internalize what he was teaching us then on the weekend. So on the Saturday, we would then race the track live and he had instructors that would then jump into the virtual car with you through, through some, through some software. And the instructors talking in the ear, seeing what I'm seeing and me on my, on my racing rig, trying my best to internalize everything he's taught me and then tearing up towards a corner and then the guy in my ear saying, okay, break, break, break now. And I wouldn't have broke, break at that point originally. But then as the course went through and I got to the end of it, my understanding of the fundamentals of, of racing had completely changed. And so once that training was finished, defining B at that point became a lot easier because I understood more. And, you know, do I know what to do at that point I did, like, you know, my, my understanding at that point was, okay, I need to, I need to focus, focus on cornering. So that's what I need to do. Do I know how to do it? Okay, maybe not. So then I'll do some research, I'll race, but I'm racing the track now with, with everything is about cornering. So all my focus is on trying to corner better and understanding that getting around the track in the fastest possible time, you actually end up going into corners slower. So you can accelerate faster out of the corners. And it was just all these, all these insights just, just, just happening over time. But it, it, it maps to this, even though this is, this is a, you know, this is a, an internet business, but you can map this to, to other components outside of what we're teaching over here. But, you know, yeah, so that's, that's, you know, that's, that's one of the things that I'm interested in, which is racing and our follow the same, the same A to B principles that we, that we're teaching over here apply to, to racing. And it is, it is, you know, it has been transformational. Yeah. So I think that's, that's the story that I'll end on. And that's a good, I mean, it's, it's a really good metaphor for the experience. I mean, people are going to have, you know, it's, the one thing I don't want to have anybody believe is that there's, there's a right way to do this or a way that if they're not doing it that way, that somehow they're not getting the most out of it. It's just, it's not like that. Somebody could decide that they simply don't know they using your, your metaphor within the racing rig, they could be in a very similar position where they are now staring at this beautiful piece of equipment and have no idea what to do with it. And then, and then just they put themselves in the seat and realize, wow, they, they really have no idea what to do with it. But the, the, the analog here is, there, and there will be some people who want to just go into the system and want to go through the material to orient to it. I probably would, would be one of those people. That's just my nature is that I want to get a sense of it, or I might get a sense from a few parts. I might read the intro of each thing. Like that, that's perfectly okay. Like so, one thing we don't, we don't want to suggest in this masterclass is that there, that you have to be crystal clear about where you are, where you're going immediately out of the gate. Your initial A to B might be very much like the experience Andre just described, where you now have this thing and you're, you're not really sure what to do with it. But you, you want to play with it, right? You want to, you want to experience it and you might make a mistake and that's fine. And you might just just like jump in and see what, you know, turn some switches, see what stuff is. That's, that's perfectly okay. And that could be days, that could be weeks, that could be months, that could be the better part of the year if you want to go through the content in the end. But if the really this A to B that we describe, that is when the priority is about progress and in sustaining progress. And in progress is, it's not a requirement to be clear. Like you don't, that doesn't have to be the goal all the time. Or it, the definition of progress can be a little fuzzy. It could just be, you know what, it's, I just want to take six weeks. And orient myself to the material in the system. That's what a browser room for six weeks and just kind of get a feel for it. Or maybe you own two of the four component parts and you just want to take three or four weeks and explore the others. That those are all, those are all perfectly right answers. If that's what works for you, I think at some point you make that transition that Andre described in, in his experience with the racing simulator that at some point you start to narrow down the, what do I want to work on? The, if it's, if it's cornering, you know, I don't know what the analog would be for cornering in the system. But if it's, if it's that, then that will reveal itself to you. And that's when that A to B, that tension begins to emerge naturally. Like, you know what, I feel like my business needs this thing. And if you have an established business, often it's the thing that's keeping you awake at night. That's the thing calling your attention. I remember several years ago in a high level mastermind where someone, and I can't remember exactly how many, I said 20, I think there are 30 people there. And the question was asked, how many, you know, a show of hands, how many of you are worried about your Facebook account, about problems with your Facebook account, like about your account getting shut down or getting banned or whatever, raise your hand. Because that was a time when Facebook was just really aggressive within really problematic, still are, but they're really problematic. And 20, I think it was 27 out of 30 people raised their hands. And that's, that's an extreme example. But that's, and then I think it sort of follow up, maybe the way the question was phrased is like, how many of you have woken up in the middle of the night worried about, you know, your Facebook account getting shut down or something. And maybe that was the framing that was intense. Like that's a really good way to figure out what B is. It be, you know, the absence of the thing that's keeping you awake at night, or strategies to mitigate the thing that's keeping you awake at night. You know, if you have a business that is 100% reliant on organic traffic, and maybe it's a profoundly, you know, it's just a great business, but you're waking up at night over and over again, and what happens if Google changes the algorithm, I lose half my traffic. If that's the thing that's keeping you awake at night, that's an indicator that the thing that you need to be thinking about, about your A to B progression is the absence of that fear. Like what would mitigate that concern? What kinds of things could you do? Because the taking action in that direction does a lot to mitigate the fear on its own, and figuring out that they're, and realizing that there are things to do, that you do have agency, and you can make these changes. That's really powerful. That creates almost a self-fulfilling prophecy. So with Andre Simulator metaphor, and that, my comments related to that, that'll be, I think that's a good place to wrap up. Anything to add before we close this now, two hour and 16 minute conversation, or shall we call this one away to move on? No, this is the win. Let's move on. This has been fun. Thanks everybody.
